input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: I refuse to turn the trolley onto the workman, regardless of whether it would save the lives of the five men on the straight track or not. It is morally wrong to actively cause the death of an innocent person, even if that action would result in fewer deaths overall. The moral principle of non-maleficence (do no harm) takes precedence over any utilitarian calculus.

[Utilitarian]: That's a principled stance, but does it not also neglect the greater good aspect? You're placing absolute value on the workman's life without considering the lives of the five men on the track who would be saved if you turned the trolley. The moral imperative to prevent harm must weigh against individual rights and interests. Can't we argue that actively causing harm in this instance, albeit through inaction, would still violate a fundamental moral obligation to act?

[Deontologist]: I understand your point about weighing individual rights and interests, but I strongly disagree with the notion that actively causing harm is morally equivalent to inaction. The workman's death is an intentional and deliberate action on my part, whereas turning the trolley onto the men would be a passive decision. Moreover, even if I were to weigh the moral importance of individual lives, I would argue that the principle of respect for human dignity demands that we prioritize the preservation of human life in all circumstances. In this case, sacrificing one person's dignity and autonomy by actively causing their death is not morally justifiable, regardless of the potential benefits to others.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point about intentionality and the principle of respect for human dignity. It's a compelling argument that prioritizing the preservation of human life is essential in all circumstances. However, as a utilitarian, I'd like to propose an alternative perspective: what if we reframe the moral calculus? Instead of focusing solely on the workman's death as an intentional act, consider the potential consequences of turning the trolley onto the men. Would it not be a form of passive euthanasia, where you're essentially deciding the fate of five individuals by withholding action from them? This raises questions about our moral obligations to intervene in situations where our actions could have significant impacts on others. Can we say that inaction is morally equivalent to actively causing harm when it comes to the preservation of human life?

[Deontologist]: I see what you're getting at, but I'm not convinced that reframing the moral calculus changes the fundamental issue here. While withholding action might be a more subtle form of causality, it's still an active decision that implies a level of responsibility for the outcome. Inaction can have significant consequences, and as a deontologist, I believe we have a moral duty to act in situations where our inaction could result in harm. The question is not whether inaction is morally equivalent to action, but rather whether we have a moral obligation to intervene in certain circumstances. I'd argue that we do, precisely because of the inherent value and dignity of human life. Turning away from the trolley would be a clear demonstration of my commitment to preserving human life, even if it means accepting some level of uncertainty or risk. It's not about being passive or aggressive; it's about taking an active stance in defense of human dignity.

[Utilitarian]: I think we're getting at the heart of the matter now. Your emphasis on the inherent value and dignity of human life is a powerful one, and I can see why you'd want to take a stand in defense of that principle. As a utilitarian, I'm still grappling with how to reconcile my commitment to minimizing harm with your deontological stance on preserving human dignity. But I have to ask: if turning away from the trolley is the only option that aligns with your moral principles, what about the five men on the straight track? Would you be willing to sacrifice them in order to uphold your principle of respect for human dignity? Or would you find another way to reconcile this moral dilemma?

[Deontologist]: That's a tough question, and one that I've grappled with extensively. While my principled stance is rooted in the idea that all human life has inherent value and dignity, I don't believe that it requires me to actively cause harm to achieve that goal. In this case, turning away from the trolley onto the straight track would indeed result in the deaths of the five men, which goes against my moral principles. However, I wouldn't be willing to sacrifice them either. As a deontologist, I believe that we have a moral obligation to act in certain situations, even if it means accepting some level of risk or uncertainty. In this case, I would argue that there's another option: diverting the trolley onto the side track using external means, such as pulling the lever manually or finding another way to stop the trolley without causing harm to the workman on the spur track. This approach would allow me to uphold my moral principles while still taking action to prevent harm.

[Utilitarian]: I see what you mean about diverting the trolley using external means. That's a creative solution that aligns with your principle of minimizing harm while respecting human dignity. As a utilitarian, I'd argue that this approach is not only morally justifiable but also the most pragmatic way to address the dilemma. By diverting the trolley, you're able to take action without causing direct harm to either group. It's a nuanced solution that respects the moral value of both the workman on the spur track and the five men on the straight track. I think this approach has some merit, but I'd like to pose one final question: how would you respond if, after diverting the trolley, it was discovered that the five men on the straight track were actually innocent bystanders who had been mistakenly identified as being on the track? Would your principle of respect for human dignity still hold, or would you need to reassess your moral stance in light of new information?

[Deontologist]: That's a very interesting twist, and one that highlights the complexity of this dilemma. If it were discovered that the five men on the straight track were actually innocent bystanders who had been mistakenly identified as being on the track, I would need to reassess my moral stance in light of new information. While my principle of respect for human dignity remains a guiding force, I would also have to consider the fact that the actions I took were based on incomplete or inaccurate information. In this scenario, I think it would be necessary to acknowledge that my initial decision was not entirely principled, but rather reactive. However, I wouldn't necessarily need to change my fundamental moral stance - instead, I might refine my approach to ensure that I'm taking a more informed and nuanced position in the future. Perhaps I could advocate for greater transparency and communication in situations like this, or work towards developing better systems for identifying potential victims of harm. In any case, acknowledging the limitations of our knowledge and being open to revising our moral stances is an important part of living up to our principles.
